title: American History X Cellent

jokes.text: Me, C. Montgomery Burns, locked up like an animal? What caused this puzzling turn of events? Well, I'll tell you my tale... by thinking about it to myself.
jokes.setup: Burns dramatically asks what caused his imprisonment
jokes.punchline: He'll tell his tale 'by thinking about it to myself' - breaking the fourth wall while being absurd
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-narrative device that's perfectly in character for Burns' dramatic self-importance

jokes.text: And so our Employee of the Month is the late Roger Ducette, who tragically died from complications due to union organizing.
jokes.setup: Normal employee of the month announcement
jokes.punchline: The employee died from 'complications due to union organizing' - implying Burns had him killed
jokes.explanation: Dark humor perfectly suited to Burns' evil character, delivered matter-of-factly

jokes.text: This picnic is for me. You will all be spending your Independence Day slaving away in the hot summer sun without pay, lotion or gratitude.
jokes.setup: Employees excited about Fourth of July picnic
jokes.punchline: Burns reveals it's only for him, they'll be working without pay, lotion, or gratitude
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns cruelty with the specific detail of 'without lotion' adding absurd specificity

jokes.text: I've played a round of tenpins or two in my life, and to me, the word 'spare' reeks of second best. Give me ten full frames of strike ribs.
jokes.setup: Burns is offered spareribs
jokes.punchline: He rejects 'spare' as second-best and demands 'strike ribs' using bowling terminology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic perfectly fitting Burns' character - the bowling metaphor is unexpected and well-constructed

jokes.text: And you, find my doctor and ask him why I'd ask for something as insane as strike ribs.
jokes.setup: Burns just demanded strike ribs
jokes.punchline: He immediately acknowledges it's insane but still wants to know why he'd ask for it
jokes.explanation: Great callback that adds self-aware absurdity - Burns knows he's being crazy but doubles down

jokes.text: I said break a leg. For God's sake, man, it's a figure of speech. You're fired.
jokes.setup: Burns tells someone to 'break a leg' before the performance
jokes.punchline: Someone literally breaks their leg, Burns clarifies it's a figure of speech, then fires them
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons literal interpretation gag with Burns' typical heartless response

jokes.text: Smithers, take my hands and clap them sarcastically.
jokes.setup: Burns wants to show sarcastic appreciation
jokes.punchline: He's so lazy/entitled he makes Smithers physically clap his hands for him
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly captures the Burns-Smithers dynamic in one absurd command - highly quotable and character-specific

jokes.text: More sarcastic.
jokes.setup: Smithers is already clapping Burns' hands sarcastically
jokes.punchline: Burns demands even more sarcasm in the clapping
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the previous gag - the idea of degrees of sarcastic clapping is absurd

jokes.text: That's a regular-sized bottle. I'm drinking Jeroboams.
jokes.setup: Lenny offers expensive wine in what seems like a large bottle
jokes.punchline: Lenny dismisses it as regular-sized and claims to be drinking Jeroboams (massive bottles)
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation gag but not particularly character-specific or memorable

jokes.text: I'm pounding Melchizedeks.
jokes.setup: Lenny drinking Jeroboams
jokes.punchline: Carl one-ups with Melchizedeks (even larger bottles) using 'pounding' as drinking term
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with biblical wine bottle name, 'pounding' adds mild wordplay

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo. I'm drinking my salary.
jokes.setup: Homer learns the wine costs $60,000
jokes.punchline: He celebrates that he's literally drinking his annual salary
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Homer-esque reaction - celebrates the absurd expense rather than being concerned

jokes.text: The government... taking my hard-earned stolen wine and spending it on that no-good floor.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about taxes on his 'salary'
jokes.punchline: He calls the stolen wine 'hard-earned' and complains about government spending on 'that no-good floor'
jokes.explanation: Great contradiction ('hard-earned stolen wine') and absurd target for government waste complaint

jokes.text: Smithers, sound the alarum. Summon the shire reeve. Wake the beadle.
jokes.setup: Burns discovers the break-in
jokes.punchline: He uses archaic medieval terms instead of calling the police
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - his age and pretension expressed through medieval vocabulary

jokes.text: Is it a crime to want nice things, and then to steal them from a public museum where any gum-chewing monkey in a Tufts University jacket can gawk at them?
jokes.setup: Burns asked to explain stolen artwork
jokes.punchline: He frames art theft as rescuing art from 'gum-chewing monkeys in Tufts University jackets'
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns elitism with specific dig at Tufts University - great character work and quotable

jokes.text: Me, whose velvet breeches their lizard lips had so often joylessly smooched.
jokes.setup: Burns describing the townspeople jeering him
jokes.punchline: Overly flowery description of people who used to kiss his ass
jokes.explanation: Burns' verbose, archaic way of saying people kissed his ass - 'joylessly smooched' is great phrasing

jokes.text: Fresh is a dollar, rotten's two bucks. Son, do you really need the rotten? I mean, it's not like you're actually gonna hit him.
jokes.setup: Vendor selling throwing produce at different prices
jokes.punchline: Rotten costs more, vendor questions if customer needs it since he won't actually hit Burns
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about riot economics and people's poor aim

jokes.text: Really? Okay, eat your lunch. But... You said it's your lunch. Eat it.
jokes.setup: Bart claims rotten fruit is his lunch to avoid getting in trouble
jokes.punchline: Marge calling his bluff by making him actually eat it
jokes.explanation: Great parent-child dynamic where Marge expertly calls Bart's lie with escalating pressure

jokes.text: Okay, eat your lunch... You said it's your lunch. Eat it... Not air bites, real bites.
jokes.setup: Bart claims rotten fruit is his lunch to avoid trouble
jokes.punchline: Marge forces him to actually eat it, specifying 'not air bites, real bites'
jokes.explanation: Classic parent-child power struggle with Marge's characteristic thoroughness

jokes.text: Mom, you don't play with Lisa. You play despite her.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests Bart play with Lisa
jokes.punchline: Bart corrects that you don't play 'with' Lisa, you play 'despite' her
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that perfectly captures sibling dynamics and Bart's attitude toward Lisa

jokes.text: One day we might need each other for organ transplants, so we'd better keep the lines of communication open.
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining why she and Bart should get along
jokes.punchline: Her practical reason is potential future organ transplants
jokes.explanation: Darkly pragmatic reasoning that fits Lisa's logical but sometimes clinical worldview

jokes.text: You plant the ants, and then they grow? No, the ants are the farmers.
jokes.setup: Bart misunderstands ant farms
jokes.punchline: Lisa's literal correction that the ants are the farmers
jokes.explanation: Simple misunderstanding gag that shows the education gap between siblings

jokes.text: After all my years of stock jobbing, gun running, attempted murder, successful murder, and tom-peepery, they get me on a petty multimillion dollar art theft.
jokes.setup: Burns lamenting the unfairness of his arrest
jokes.punchline: Lists increasingly serious crimes, then calls multimillion dollar art theft 'petty'
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Burns' crimes with perfect ironic perspective on what's 'petty'

jokes.text: Hmm. Not dramatic enough. Frostbite took his nose. Excellent.
jokes.setup: Burns continuing to embellish his story
jokes.punchline: He adds increasingly dramatic elements, pleased with the frostbite detail
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the narrative manipulation joke, showing Burns' vanity

jokes.text: That prison was like a prison to me. Not a prison of bricks and iron, but one of steel and cement.
jokes.setup: Burns describing prison metaphorically
jokes.punchline: He clarifies it wasn't a prison of bricks and iron, but of steel and cement - still just a prison
jokes.explanation: Burns' attempt at profound metaphor that just describes a regular prison with different materials

jokes.text: That's just an S.S. card, you Dummkopf.
jokes.setup: Guards find Burns' Social Security card
jokes.punchline: Burns calls it an 'S.S. card' using Nazi terminology
jokes.explanation: Dark humor playing on Burns' Nazi connections, though somewhat predictable for his character

jokes.text: Can't read my own writing. But I'll tell you this, Warden, it starts with an 'H.'
jokes.setup: Burns claims to have damaging information about the warden
jokes.punchline: He can't read his own writing but knows it starts with 'H'
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Burns' threat - his illiteracy undermines his blackmail attempt

jokes.text: Damn, that gas is noble.
jokes.setup: Burns in a science lab/chemistry setting
jokes.punchline: He comments that the gas is 'noble' - chemistry pun on noble gases
jokes.explanation: Simple chemistry pun that's more clever than funny - fits Burns' education but not very impactful

jokes.text: I haven't had a cavity in over 40 years. I wasn't talking about your teeth. Nor was I.
jokes.setup: Guard announces cavity search, Burns mentions his dental health
jokes.punchline: Guard clarifies it's not dental, Burns reveals he wasn't talking about teeth either
jokes.explanation: Double misdirection with uncomfortable implications - decent structure but predictable territory

jokes.text: Dartmouth?! Get me away from this hideous brute at once! Got my master's from the University of Virginia, the public Ivy. Guard!
jokes.setup: Burns meets seemingly harmless white-collar cellmate from Dartmouth
jokes.punchline: Burns is horrified by Dartmouth, even more so by UVA being called 'public Ivy'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns elitist snobbery - even among criminals he maintains academic hierarchy prejudice

jokes.text: Well, they don't turn out too many of those at Dartmouth.
jokes.setup: Burns afraid of tattooed bruisers
jokes.punchline: Cellmate reveals he went to Dartmouth, subverting expectations
jokes.explanation: Standard class-based misdirection - competent but not particularly original

jokes.text: Got my master's from the University of Virginia, the public Ivy.
jokes.setup: Cellmate already mentioned Dartmouth
jokes.punchline: He adds UVA credentials and defensively calls it 'the public Ivy'
jokes.explanation: Nice character detail showing insecurity about public vs. private education

jokes.text: If I was in your tummy, I'd poo in your throat.
jokes.setup: Bart and Lisa arguing about parentage
jokes.punchline: Bart's gross-out threat from inside Lisa's hypothetical womb
jokes.explanation: Typical Bart grossout humor - in character but relies on scatological shock value

jokes.text: Oh, no. You are not taking the children. If they're anybody's children, they're mine.
jokes.setup: Ants escaping from the ant farm
jokes.punchline: Homer claiming ownership of the ants as 'his children'
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic attachment to random things treated as family members

jokes.text: A chance to be the kind of boss I want to be. A little more... employee-friendly.
jokes.setup: Smithers taking over for Burns
jokes.punchline: His idea of reform is being 'a little more employee-friendly'
jokes.explanation: Shows Smithers' limited imagination for reform after years under Burns

jokes.text: A suggestion box that is no longer a guillotine for the hand.
jokes.setup: Smithers announcing workplace improvements
jokes.punchline: The old suggestion box was literally a hand guillotine
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor revealing Burns' extreme workplace hostility - highly quotable

jokes.text: There's always a catch!
jokes.setup: Worker asking about the catch to workplace improvements
jokes.punchline: After Smithers explains the catch, worker declares there's always a catch
jokes.explanation: Weak payoff to the setup - predictable worker cynicism without much wit

jokes.text: Annie? As in 'Little Orphan'?
jokes.setup: Lisa names the remaining ant Annie
jokes.punchline: Homer makes the obvious Little Orphan Annie connection
jokes.explanation: Obvious cultural reference without any twist or cleverness

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! A double funeral!
jokes.setup: Homer learns Patty and Selma might be dead
jokes.punchline: He celebrates the prospect of their double funeral
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer heartlessness toward his in-laws - perfectly in character dark humor

jokes.text: Five out of five. Four out of five. Five out of five. Uh, excuse me, can I go back and change that four out of five to five out of five?
jokes.setup: Lenny giving service ratings
jokes.punchline: His obsessive need to change a 4/5 to 5/5 rating
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about rating culture and people-pleasing behavior

jokes.text: I don't have a thing with my kid tomorrow. You told me you did. I do, but I'm not going to it.
jokes.setup: Worker admits lying about kid's event
jokes.punchline: Reveals he does have an event but won't attend anyway
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing worker's callousness - the logic is amusingly twisted

jokes.text: There's nothing sweeter than being nice to a guy's face and then stabbing him in the back the minute he walks away.
jokes.setup: Workers celebrating Smithers
jokes.punchline: Worker reveals his philosophy of fake niceness and backstabbing
jokes.explanation: Dark observation about workplace dynamics - honest but not particularly clever

jokes.text: I used to wonder why Mr. Burns hated humanity. Now I know. You open your heart and they mock your very decency.
jokes.setup: Smithers trusted the workers
jokes.punchline: He now understands Burns' misanthropy through personal betrayal
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing Smithers' disillusionment - well-structured emotional beat

jokes.text: Because when I was at my lowest facing 50 years to life, someone handed me a book. I couldn't read a lick, but that picture of Jesus gave me a comfort I've never known.
jokes.setup: Convict explaining his religious conversion
jokes.punchline: He's illiterate but was comforted by Jesus picture
jokes.explanation: Gentle mockery of prison conversions while showing genuine human need for comfort

jokes.text: I'm afraid that was just my pre-evil.
jokes.setup: Convict thinks he's sucked out Burns' evil
jokes.punchline: Burns reveals that was just his 'pre-evil'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Burns has layers of evil like an onion. 'Pre-evil' is a perfect coined term

jokes.text: She's only carrying 30 times her weight. Our girl is slowing down.
jokes.setup: Lisa observing her ant's strength
jokes.punchline: 30 times bodyweight is considered slowing down for an ant
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa precision about ant facts that shows her overattachment to the pet

jokes.text: Or let her die in captivity, like Grampa? Hey, in my mind I'm free. No you're not, ya idjit!
jokes.setup: Lisa pondering letting the ant die free vs captive
jokes.punchline: Grampa's delusion of freedom immediately crushed by Homer
jokes.explanation: Great dark family moment where Homer crushes his father's only comfort

jokes.text: If wishes were horses, I'd be eating wish-meat every night. What does that mean?
jokes.setup: Carl mangles the 'if wishes were horses' saying
jokes.punchline: His version involves eating 'wish-meat,' Lenny asks what it means
jokes.explanation: Great malapropism that creates an absurd new saying - the confusion is perfectly delivered

jokes.text: But first, I'll daydream about a sport utility vehicle, a crispy chicken sandwich and a wonderful blue pill.
jokes.setup: Burns about to continue his story
jokes.punchline: He'll daydream about commercial products first (obvious ad break reference)
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about TV commercials - clever but not particularly funny or memorable

jokes.text: You don't have a 'whisper' voice, do you?
jokes.setup: Convict speaking at normal volume about secret details
jokes.punchline: Burns points out the convict can't whisper
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about the convict's inability to be subtle

jokes.text: Oh, thank God. I thought I broke two off.
jokes.setup: Lisa corrects that ants have six legs, not eight
jokes.punchline: Bart's relieved he didn't break off legs, showing his confusion
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's confusion and relief at not being destructive

jokes.text: Men of wealth can beat the system now. Yeah, and they've replaced parking meters with this thing you stick your credit card into.
jokes.setup: Explaining how the world has changed
jokes.punchline: Credit card parking meters presented as equally significant as wealthy privilege
jokes.explanation: Mild satire about technological change being treated as equally important as social issues

jokes.text: I had a little left between my toes. It quickly multiplied, and now I'm a bigger bastard than ever.
jokes.setup: Convict asks why Burns is leaving if he was cleansed
jokes.punchline: Evil was hiding between his toes and multiplied like bacteria
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist explanation treating evil like a fungal infection - perfectly twisted Burns logic

jokes.text: Because I've killed so many rich white men, I thought I'd do right by at least one.
jokes.setup: Burns asks why the convict tried to save him
jokes.punchline: Convict's guilty conscience about killing rich white men
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about serial killing with twisted moral reasoning - good character consistency

jokes.text: Tell me, brother, have you heard the story of Jesus Christ? Well, I am Italian, but no.
jokes.setup: Convict asks if man has heard of Jesus
jokes.punchline: Italian man says he's Italian but doesn't know Jesus story
jokes.explanation: Good irony about Italian not knowing Christianity despite Vatican connection - solid ending gag

tmdb_overview: When the police are called to diffuse a rowdy crowd at Mr. Burns' estate, one of the officers recognizes priceless stolen paintings on the walls, and the maniacal billionaire is taken downtown for questioning. With Mr. Burns gone, Smithers takes charge of the power plant. But when employees take advantage of his good nature, he exacts revenge by forcing the employees to work night and day. These unfair working conditions prompt Homer and his crew to devise a plan to bust out Mr. Burns.
